support.title: Support development
support.intro: This is completely optional — TreeMonk will stay free forever. You're only supporting its development. It's not expected at all, but thank you so much if you choose to send something!
support.scan: Scan the QR code or tap the link

photo.dragHint: Drag to reposition, use the slider to zoom.

places.title: Places
places.hint: Place hierarchy (village → district → county → country) and GOV id. The place names stored on people stay untouched.
places.select: Pick a place from the list.
places.standardizeDesc: Rewrite every place name to its canonical form (merges duplicates like “…Hungary” / “…Magyarország”).

welcome.title: How do you like TreeMonk so far?
welcome.intro: Tell us in a few seconds how happy you are — it really helps shape the app!
welcome.privacyNote: Only your rating, your note and the app version are sent — never any family-tree data.

feedback.title: Send feedback
feedback.desc: Found a bug or have an idea? Tell the developer — no account needed.
feedback.intro: Found a bug or have an idea? Drop a message — it goes straight to the developer.
feedback.messagePlaceholder: Describe what happened, or what you'd like to see…
feedback.emailPlaceholder: name@example.com — only if you'd like a reply
feedback.privacyNote: App version, OS and language are attached automatically (v{{version}}). No tree or personal data is ever sent.
feedback.error: Couldn't send feedback. Please try again later.
feedback.errorNetwork: No connection. Check your internet and try again.

cmd.placeholder: Search people or type a command…

attach.title: Attach to others too?
attach.subtitle: Attached:
attach.noRelatives: No close relatives to quick-pick — use the search.
attach.pickSubtitle: Pick a document from the library to attach to this person.
attach.pickEmpty: No documents to link (everything is already attached, or nothing matches the search).

quality.title: Data quality
quality.dashboardHint: Average completeness of the people's records.

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.subtitle: A summary of your whole tree
dashboard.includeSpousesHint: Include married-in spouses in the statistics
dashboard.scopeEmpty: No one in this view. Try a different reference person or scope.
dashboard.migrationDesc: Birth → death place, the most common routes
dashboard.migrationNone: Not enough place data to show migration

update.installHint: Run the downloaded installer to update to the latest version. Your data is kept.
update.downloadStarted: The download opened in your browser.

chat.title: Chat with the developer
chat.subtitle: A question, bug or idea? Write to us!
chat.privacy: Anonymous — only what you type here is sent. No family-tree data ever leaves the app.
chat.emptyBody: Send us any question, bug or idea — we try to reply quickly!
chat.consentBody: Before you write, here's how the chat handles your data:
chat.consentPoint1: Only the messages you type are sent — with an anonymous, random id (per install) and technical context (app version, OS, language).
chat.consentPoint2: The chat data (only your messages and the above) is stored on TreeMonk's own server (treemonk.eu) so we can reply — no third parties.
chat.consentPoint3: The id is not linked to your identity, and no family-tree or personal data ever leaves the app.
chat.deleteConfirm: Delete the whole conversation? This removes it from the server and your device — it can't be undone.

todos.title: To-dos
todos.empty: No tasks yet. Create one and link the people it concerns.
todos.personEmpty: No to-dos are linked to this person.
todos.deleteConfirm: Delete this to-do? It will also be unlinked from any people.

calendar.title: Calendar
calendar.subtitle: Birthdays, deaths & marriages by day — across every year.

audit.title: History
audit.subtitle: Every change in order — and undoable.
audit.confirmBody: Undo this change? The previous state will be restored.

warn.laterEdits: You edited this item {{count}} more time(s) afterwards — undoing will overwrite those.
warn.cascade: Undoing also removes {{count}} related record(s) (families, children, events…).

famous.title: Famous relatives
famous.subtitle: Possible notable people in your tree
famous.empty: No famous matches found in your tree.
famous.disclaimer: These are POSSIBLE matches by name + birth year — verify with the relationship path that it's really them.
famous.customEmpty: No custom famous people added yet.
famous.dbEmpty: No such famous person in the database.

kinship.title: Relationship finder
kinship.subtitle: How two people are connected
kinship.intro: Pick two people and I'll show the kinship line between them — with a pulsing light beam along the connection.
kinship.none: No known relationship between these two people.

issues.givenNamesHint: Spelling/accent variants of the same given name. Pick or type the spelling to keep, then unify.
issues.title: Data Issues
issues.subtitle: Biological and logical inconsistencies
issues.duplicatesHint: Likely the same person entered twice.
issues.surnamesHint: Spelling/accent variants of the same surname. Pick or type the spelling to keep, then unify.
issues.mergeHint: Every relationship, source and attachment of the duplicate moves to the survivor; the duplicate's name is kept as an alias. Reversible.
issues.mergeWarnParents: Different parents — the merged person may end up with two parent couples.
issues.clean: No data issues found. Everything checks out!
issues.dismissHint: Hide permanently — this isn't a real error
issues.dismissBody: Hide this anomaly? It will be permanently dismissed as a false positive and won't appear in the scan again.

rules.siblingsTooClose: Siblings born less than 8 months apart
rules.probablyDeceased: Probably deceased (marked living but born long ago)

fix.markDeceasedBody: Mark {{name}} as deceased? You can optionally add a death date, or leave it blank.

settings.subtitle: Personalize, manage your data and backups
settings.reduceEffectsDesc: Turn off the glass blur for solid surfaces — far less CPU on weak / non-GPU-accelerated machines
settings.famousDesc: Add your own famous people for the scan (matched by name + birth year).
settings.backupDesc: Save the database and media to a .zip file
settings.restoreWarning: Your current data will be overwritten from the backup and the app will restart. Continue?
settings.resetDesc: Permanently delete all people, families, sources and boards
settings.resetWarning: This will PERMANENTLY delete ALL data (people, families, sources, boards). The app will restart. This cannot be undone!
settings.gedcomExportDesc: Write the database to a .ged file
settings.verificationDesc: A green/orange mark on every person (verified / not verified) — toggle it from any person card

api.title: Local API
api.enableDesc: HTTP access to your family tree — strictly on this machine (127.0.0.1), token-protected.
api.tokenDesc: Required on every request: Authorization: Bearer <token>
api.writesDesc: Create, edit and delete through the API. Use with care!
api.docsDesc: Built-in API reference and examples (works offline).
api.regenerated: New token generated — the old one is now invalid.

settings.manualDesc: The full TreeMonk manual as a PDF, opened in your default viewer.
settings.siteExportDesc: A browsable, searchable HTML page of the whole tree — a single file you can share with anyone. Confidential people are excluded.
settings.csvImportDesc: Bulk person entry from a spreadsheet (Excel/CSV). Columns are recognised by header: surname, given name, sex, birth/death date and place, occupation…
settings.indexExportDesc: Printable alphabetical name index and place index (who was born/married/died where) as a single HTML file.

workspace.label: Tree
workspace.deleteConsequence: This removes the tree from the list. The data file is kept on disk and can be re-added later.

delete.docConsequence: Deleting it will remove it from these people too.
delete.docUnattached: This document is not attached to anyone.
delete.personConsequence: Deleting this person will also remove:
delete.itemBoard: Removal from the investigation board (if present)
delete.undoHint: You can undo this from the notification that appears.

board.title: Investigation Board
board.subtitle: A free-form canvas to uncover connections

wizard.title: Investigation Wizard

pickFor.father: Whose father are you looking for?
pickFor.mother: Whose mother are you looking for?
pickFor.child: Whose child are you looking for?
pickFor.spouse: Whose spouse are you looking for?

wizard.noPeople: No people in the database yet.

mysteryDesc.father: {{name}}'s father is unknown — connect the evidence!
mysteryDesc.mother: {{name}}'s mother is unknown — connect the evidence!
mysteryDesc.child: Looking for a missing child of {{name}} — gather the clues!
mysteryDesc.spouse: {{name}}'s spouse is unknown — gather the clues!

guide.father: To find the father, check {{name}}'s baptism record ({{place}}, around {{year}}) — the father's name and occupation are usually listed. Cross-reference the siblings' parish entries too.
guide.mother: To find the mother, look for {{name}}'s baptism record ({{place}}, around {{year}}); the mother's name (often her maiden name) appears there. Godparents can be a lead as well.
guide.child: For {{name}}'s children, comb the baptism registers near {{place}} in the years after the marriage, plus census / household lists.
guide.spouse: To find the spouse, look for the marriage record ({{place}}, around {{year}}) and the children's baptisms — the other parent's name appears there.
guide.findMarriage: No marriage recorded between {{spouse}} and {{name}} — find the marriage register first ({{place}}, around {{year}}); it often names the parents and the prior residence.

records.birth: Registers: baptism / birth entry for {{place}} (around {{year}}). Check both the parish and the civil registry.
records.marriage: Marriage register ({{place}}, around {{year}}): witnesses and parents are often named. Search the years before the first child's birth.
records.death: Death register and cemetery records for {{place}} (around {{year}}). The entry often gives the age and the parents.

board.clearHint: Drag elements here and connect them
board.dropHint: Drop images or files here, or paste a screenshot (Ctrl/Cmd+V)
board.empty: The board is empty — add an element from the top bar

tree.title: Family Tree
tree.subtitle: Hierarchical view of verified family members
tree.empty: No family members yet. Import a GEDCOM file or merge from the board.

map.title: Genealogy Map
map.subtitle: Birth, death and marriage locations
map.empty: No location data. Import a GEDCOM file that contains coordinates.
map.rangeIgnored: Showing the full lifepath of the selected person
map.bloodlineOnlyHint: Only the starting person's blood relatives (drop married-in people and their ancestry)
map.offline: Map tiles couldn't load — check your internet connection.

person.anomaliesTitle: {{count}} possible data issue(s) for this person
person.copyBirthToChristening: Use birth date & place for christening
person.sourcesDrop: Drop files/images here, or use File / Link to attach sources
person.fsIdInvalid: Not a valid FamilySearch ID format (e.g. LZ1B-2CD).
person.name: Name
person.unlinkSpouseBody: Only the marriage link is removed — the person is NOT deleted and can be attached to another marriage afterwards.
person.unlinkSpouseChildren: The {{count}} children stay with this person (as a single-parent family).
person.givenNamesHint: Type a name; space or Enter starts the next one; drag the chips to reorder — the number shows each position. Mark the one they went by in the Call name field.
person.unlinkParentsBody: Only the child–parents link is removed — no person is deleted.

research.empty: No research logged yet. Track searches — even dead ends — so you never repeat them.

timeline.worldEventsHint: Show the major world events during this person's life

documents.subtitle: Photos, registry records and papers

documents.empty: No documents. Import one to get started.

gedcom.importingHint: Large files can take a while — please wait.
gedcom.exportSubtitle: Choose who and what goes into the file.
gedcom.exportRootHint: Can be anyone — not just the tree's root. E.g. the cousin you're sending it to.
gedcom.scopeBloodDesc: Only the starting person's blood relatives.
gedcom.scopeAncestorsDesc: The starting person and their ancestors.
gedcom.scopeDescendantsDesc: The starting person and their descendants.
gedcom.exportIncludeSpousesHint: Also include spouses who married into the selected people.
gedcom.summaryHint: Photo downloads and place processing continue in the background.
gedcom.excludePrivateHint: People marked confidential (and links pointing at them) are left out of the file entirely.
gedcom.anonymizeLivingHint: Living people keep their place in the structure but export without name or facts ("Living") — their marriage details are withheld too.

fs.interrupted: A previous import was interrupted. You may want to clean up the empty entries it left.
fs.cleanupDesc: Delete nameless, data-less people and empty families left behind by imports.
fs.title: FamilySearch
fs.desc: Sign in and sync with the official FamilySearch API.
fs.noClientId: The FamilySearch developer key (AppKey) isn't configured in this build yet.
fs.signInHelp: Sign-in happens on FamilySearch's own page, in your system browser. TreeMonk never sees your password.
fs.generationsHint: How many ancestor generations to import (max 8).
fs.signInFirst: Please sign in to FamilySearch first (Settings → FamilySearch).
fs.syncNotFound: This person was not found on FamilySearch.
fs.syncedNoChange: Already up to date — no changes.
fs.pullIntro: The fields below will be updated with FamilySearch data. Local values are overwritten — photos, notes and sources are added.
fs.pushIntro: The local data below will be uploaded to FamilySearch. Only missing facts are created — nothing is overwritten.
fs.noChanges: No differences — everything is up to date.
fs.generationsHint2: Ancestor generations (unlimited) and descendant generations (children, grandchildren…). Both are fetched in rounds.
fs.updateAvailable: FamilySearch update available — click for details
fs.pushWarning: Warning: FamilySearch is a SHARED, collaborative family tree. These changes go live, are visible to other researchers, and may overwrite others' work. You are responsible for what you upload. Only continue if you are sure about them ({{name}}).
fs.pushCount: {{count}} change(s) will be uploaded to FamilySearch:
fs.pushAck: I understand I am editing a shared tree and take responsibility for these changes.
fs.importBackground: The import continues in the background.
fs.startPersonHint: The import starts here: ancestors up, descendants down (by depth). Empty = automatic starting person.
fs.startNotFound: No such FamilySearch person — check the ID.
fs.ancExplain: {{n}} generation(s) up: parents, grandparents, great-grandparents… and their spouses.
fs.descExplain: {{n}} generation(s) down: children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren… and their spouses.
fs.alwaysIncluded: The starting person, their marriage(s) and children are always included.
fs.rootPersonHint: The whole tree is imported — this only sets which person the tree view centers on.
fs.wholeTreeNote: For a personal tree the ENTIRE tree is imported (every person). Depth only applies to the shared tree.
fs.depthWarning: This could be a lot of people! The import stops at {{max}} — direct ancestors and their families take priority.
fs.priorityNote: Priority: direct ancestors → their children → descendants.

demo.introSubtitle: A preview, right in your browser
demo.introBody: This is a hands-on demo with fictional sample data. Take a look at what TreeMonk can do!
demo.introSample: A fictional sample family — not real people's data.
demo.introReadonly: Read-only — nothing can be changed, added or imported.
demo.introExplore: Explore freely: family tree, map, timeline, dashboard.
demo.readonlyToast: The demo is read-only — the sample data can't be changed.

export.title: Export family tree
export.subtitle: Save a printable, large-format family tree as vector or PDF.

general.title: General
general.intro: TreeMonk's main areas and the controls available everywhere. The left sidebar switches views; the top bar holds global actions and settings.

blocks.p: Each view is an icon in the left sidebar: Investigation Board, Family Tree, Map, People, Documents, Data issues, Query, and Settings at the bottom. Hover an icon to see its name. On the left of the top bar is the workspace switcher — you can keep several completely separate family trees and switch between them at any time.
From the top bar you can import and export GEDCOM files, the standard exchange format between genealogy programs. More advanced data handling — backup and restore — lives in Settings.
In the top bar, the sun/moon icon toggles the light and dark theme, and next to it you set the interface language (Hungarian, English or German).
Ctrl/Cmd + K opens the command palette to jump instantly to a view, action or person. F1 — or the ? button in the top bar — opens this manual at any time.

board.title: Investigation Board
board.intro: An infinite, zoomable canvas where you freely arrange your leads — notes, people, documents and images — and connect them with threads. It is a space for thinking and discovery; once a connection is certain, the "Merge to Tree" button promotes it into the permanent database.

blocks.p: Insert a new item with the buttons in the top toolbar: note, person, mystery (unknown person), evidence/image, link and zone. The item appears in the centre of the canvas, from where you drag it into place. You can also drag files straight onto the canvas from your file manager, and paste an image from the clipboard with Ctrl/Cmd + V.
Move an item by simply dragging it. A zone is a translucent frame that groups related items together — when you move the zone, the items inside move with it.

To link two items, move the mouse to the edge of one card: a small connection point appears, and dragging from it to another card draws a thread. The thread marks the relationship between the two leads.

RIGHT-click an existing item to open its context menu: edit, size (S/M/L), colour (for notes) and delete. Double-click to quickly rename a note, mystery or zone; on a person it opens the profile, and on a document or image it opens the file in your system's default application.

Click a thread to open its menu, where you set the certainty of the relationship — verified, suspicion or theory — which is also reflected in the line style; you can give it a label and a colour, or delete it.

When you click a card, the items directly connected to it stay highlighted while the rest fade — so you grasp a person's connections at a glance; click empty space to clear it. Scroll to zoom, drag empty space to pan, and use the mini-map at the bottom-right to orient yourself. The toolbar switches between corkboard and flat looks, auto-arranges the board, and the tabs at the top let you create, rename or delete several separate boards.
Delete or Backspace removes the selected item or thread. Ctrl/Cmd + V pastes an image from the clipboard as evidence.

tree.title: Family Tree
tree.intro: An overview of the verified members in three layouts: Pedigree (a side-on ancestor chart), Fan (a radial ancestor chart) and Hierarchy (a top-down tree). Switch between them at the top-left.

blocks.p: Click any person to open their profile. Drag empty space to pan, scroll to zoom, and double-click to reset the view. The "Reset to root" button at the bottom-right returns to the starting person.

The small arrow on a card's edge opens or collapses a single generation: ancestors branch to the right, descendants to the left. At the top centre, "Expand all" and "Collapse all" handle the whole tree, while "Paternal line" and "Maternal line" expand just one direct line of the starting person.

The "+" buttons on the cards let you add a father, mother, spouse or child right in the tree (you type the name in a small dialog). If someone has several marriages, the ⚭ icon switches between them, and clicking the "Children (n)" row opens the list of children.

In the fan, clicking the central disc or a wedge recentres the tree there; hovering shows the name and years, and the +/- buttons set the number of generations. In the hierarchy view, the arrow under a card expands or collapses its immediate children.

The settings panel in the top-right corner (open by default) lets you fine-tune the tree: generation spacing, accent colour, the thickness and opacity of the connector lines, the canvas background, and contrast and brightness. Every setting is saved automatically; the panel can be reset with the ↺ button and collapsed with the ▴ button.

The printer icon turns the tree into an image: choose the pedigree or fan layout, SVG or PDF format, and decide whether it goes on a single giant sheet or several A4–A0 tiles you can join together — so you can even print a large, wall-sized family tree.

dashboard.title: Dashboard
dashboard.intro: A standalone summary page in the left sidebar. It shows statistics computed from the whole database as widgets — nothing to configure, it refreshes automatically.

blocks.p: Key figures (people, families, share living, time span), sex and living/deceased breakdowns, records (oldest ancestor, longest life, most children, largest family, youngest/oldest parent), the most common surnames, birthplaces and occupations, births by century, and data completeness.
Drag any section by the handle (⋮⋮) in its header to reorder it — the layout is remembered. The “Reset layout” button restores the default order. Only widgets with meaningful data are shown.
Where a card names a specific person (e.g. oldest ancestor or longest life), clicking it opens that person's profile.

map.title: Map
map.intro: A spatial view of life events — births, marriages and deaths — with a built-in time machine. A pin's colour shows the event type: birth is green, marriage indigo, death red.

blocks.p: Drag to pan and scroll to zoom; in the 3D city view you can also drag to tilt and rotate. The compass button at the top-right resets to the default view. At the top you switch between the two base maps — 3D city and flat.

Click a pin to open that person's profile; hovering shows the name, place and year. Where many events fall on one spot, they are gathered into a numbered bubble (cluster) — clicking it zooms the map in and breaks it apart.
Type a name into the search box in the left panel to pick a person: the map jumps to their events and highlights each one with a golden halo. The X next to the search clears the selection.

The event filters (birth/marriage/death) toggle each type on and off — and double as the colour legend. The two handles of the time-machine slider set a year range, so only events from that period are shown. The surname chips let you filter to a single family's events.

The "Migration" toggle links a person's events with animated arcs in chronological order, showing their life's journey on the map. If imported place names don't yet have coordinates, the "Geocode places" button looks up positions for them so they appear as pins.

The "Manage places" button on the right panel opens the gazetteer: every known place can carry a type (village, town, district, county, country…), a parent place and a GOV id (gov.genealogy.net). The hierarchy chain shows in the editor; place names stored on people stay untouched. The Settings "Standardize place names" button rewrites long, district-laden place names to a concise settlement–county–country form.

people.title: People
people.intro: Browse and search every person in the records, and create new ones.

blocks.p: People are shown as cards that you scroll through. Type into the search box to filter by name — the search also takes aliases and name variants into account.
Click a card to open that person's detailed profile. The add button at the top creates a new, empty person whose details you then fill in on the profile.

documents.title: Documents
documents.intro: A single place for your genealogical documents — photographs, certificates, PDFs and web links.

blocks.p: Documents appear as thumbnails; clicking one opens a full-size viewer where you scroll to zoom and drag to pan across the details. The viewer's buttons also let you zoom in, zoom out, or fit the whole image to the window.
The import button adds files to the collection. Hovering over a document card reveals a trash icon you use to delete it.

person.title: Profile panel
person.intro: All of a person's data on four tabs: Overview, Family, Sources and Research. The panel slides in from the right; close it with the X in the header, and use "Show tree" to view the person in the family tree.

blocks.p: Here you edit the name, sex, birth/christening/death dates and places, religion and notes. The "Deceased" checkbox marks someone as dead even without a date. You can add several occupations, each with a from–to period. Fields save automatically as soon as you leave them. To change the photo, hover the avatar and click the camera icon. You can also add name variants (aliases) here, and the delete button is at the bottom.
The "How related to …" button at the top of a person's Overview opens the Relationship finder between the root person and this person.

The Family tab shows the parents, spouses and children; click any name to switch to their profile. Here you can add a new spouse or child, and on the heart row under a spouse you set or change the marriage date and place.

On the Sources tab you attach web links and files to the person (you can also drag files onto the marked area). Click a thumbnail to open the document, or hover it to detach it. Imported source citations also appear here, and you can copy their text.

The Research tab keeps a research log: for each entry you record a title, date, the archive searched and the result (negative, positive or inconclusive). This helps you track where you have already looked for the person — including the searches that found nothing.

The given-name field takes every given name separated by spaces; the Call name field marks the one the person actually went by (shown in quotes in the profile header). Name variants can carry a type — married name, birth (maiden) name, nickname or religious name — and the married/birth names also appear in the header. The rarer name prefix/suffix (Dr., Jr.) fields open via the "More name fields" link.

Godparents and christening witnesses are managed on the Family tab, referencing existing people. Marriage witnesses live on the specific marriage card. Any event can also list participants with free-form roles (priest, midwife, registrar…) — click the event row and use the bottom of the editor. The participant's own profile shows a "Participations" block.

On the Family tab a missing parent can be filled in (Add father/mother), and a second parent pair can be added — e.g. adoptive parents alongside birth parents. The child–parent relation is set PER PARENT (birth, adoptive, foster, step) via the pencil icon next to a child row; non-birth relations show as amber badges. The couple's own relationship is set in the marriage-line editor: marriage, partners, no relationship (a shared child without a couple), or other.

Date fields understand qualifiers: "abt 1850", "before 1850", "after 1850", "between 1850 and 1860" — stored in a standard form and displayed in your language. They sort correctly and export to GEDCOM as standard qualified dates. Julian-calendar dates take the "(J)" marker (e.g. "1700-02-11 (J)"), and dual years the "1699/1700" form.

The Other facts block stores free-form facts (height, DNA haplogroup, service number…). The stillborn flag automatically marks the person deceased; the confidential flag exports as "confidential" in GEDCOM and excludes the person from the website export.

famous.title: Famous relatives
famous.intro: Scans your whole tree and flags people who resemble a known historical figure — by name and birth year.

blocks.p: It compares the people in your tree against a bundled, freely-licensed dataset of globally famous people (from the Pantheon project) — offline, by name + birth year. Matches are ranked by fame.

These are POSSIBLE matches: a shared name can be a coincidence. Use "Wikipedia" to open the suspected person's article, and "Relationship" to see in the Relationship finder exactly how they connect to you. Confirm a match with the green check, or mark it "Manually rejected" with the red X.

The "Database" tab lets you browse the whole bundled famous-people list (with search), add your own famous people (name + birth year + optional occupation/URL), and remove your own. Added entries are merged into detection at high fame. (This editor used to live in Settings.)

kinship.title: Relationship finder
kinship.intro: Shows how two people in the tree are connected. Useful in a large, deep tree when you don't know how someone relates to you or to another person.

blocks.p: Choose the Start and the Target at the top. Type to search — fast even with 100k+ people — and each result shows identifying detail (years, birthplace) so you can tell apart people with the same name. The Start defaults to the root person.

Press "Find path" and the app finds the shortest kinship chain (through parent/child/spouse links) and draws it with the same cards and canvas as the pedigree. A pulsing light beam runs along the connection; each connector is labelled with the relation (↑ parent of, ↓ child of, ↔ spouse of). Click a card to open the profile.
On any person's page the "How related to …" button jumps here in one click, with both ends pre-filled and the search already run.

Each card on the path carries a label at the top showing how that person relates to the Start — e.g. grandfather, cousin, great-grandchild, or "… (by marriage)" for in-laws. So you can tell at a glance who's who relative to you.

issues.title: Data issues
issues.intro: This view automatically scans the database and collects likely errors — such as illogical dates or missing relationships — so you can fix them quickly.

blocks.p: Run the scan at any time with the rescan button. Each issue lists the people involved; click a name to jump straight to their profile, where you can correct the problem. The same issues also appear at the top of a person's Overview tab (this is what the "!" on the pedigree marks).

If a flag isn't actually an error, the "False positive" button — after a confirmation dialog — hides it permanently: that anomaly won't show up in the scan again. (People and data are left untouched; you only silence the warning.)

query.title: Query
query.intro: A powerful search tool that filters the records for the people you are looking for, based on conditions.

blocks.p: You build the search from rule rows: each row has a field (e.g. surname, birth place, birth year), an operator (contains, equals, less than, etc.) and a value. You can add several rules, and the AND/OR toggle decides whether all conditions or any of them must match.
Run the search with the run button, or by pressing Enter in the value field. Results appear in a table; click a row to open that person's profile.

settings.title: Settings
settings.intro: The central place for customising the interface and managing your data.

blocks.p: Set the font size, turn animations on or off, and choose how dates are displayed.
From here you can create a full backup that saves the whole database, together with attached images, into one file you can later restore. You can also export and import in GEDCOM format.
A destructive action is also available that permanently deletes ALL data for a clean slate. It cannot be undone, so it asks for explicit confirmation.
The whole tree can be exported into a single searchable HTML file that opens in any browser and can be shared with anyone — person pages cross-link, and people marked confidential are excluded.

blocks.h: CSV import, indexes and protected export
blocks.p: CSV import creates people in bulk from a spreadsheet — columns are recognised by their headers (surname, given name, sex, birth/death data…). The Name & place index produces printable alphabetical lists as a single HTML file. The GEDCOM export offers two protection options: excluding confidential people entirely, or anonymizing the living (they keep their place in the structure, but without name or facts).

changelog.title: What's new
changelog.subtitle: Every version's changes, pulled automatically from GitHub
changelog.loadFailed: Couldn't fetch releases (offline, or GitHub is unavailable).
changelog.newSince_one: {{count}} new version since your current one (v{{version}})
changelog.newSince_other: {{count}} new versions since your current one (v{{version}})

tour.title: Tour mode
tour.intro: A guided journey across the map, using the historical base map. Pick a route and a starting person.

patriline.title: Follow the bloodline — paternal line
patriline.desc: The chain of your paternal-line ancestors, from the oldest known one down to you.
patriline.hint: We travel the start person's paternal line: starting from their oldest male ancestor and stepping forward all the way to them.

tour.moreSoon: More routes coming soon (maternal line, migrations, marriages…).

supportInvite.title: Support my work
supportInvite.body: TreeMonk is free, and it always will be. Supporting it is not required and not expected — but if you feel like it, a small contribution helps the work, the development and the testing. I'm very grateful for it!
supportInvite.transferHint: On the link you can simply transfer, or scan the QR code.
supportInvite.anytime: You can support anytime: the ❤ button at the top of the left sidebar.

fsAnnounce.title: The new FamilySearch connection is coming
fsAnnounce.body: We're working on the new FamilySearch API connection, so you'll be able to reach your tree securely through FamilySearch's own sign-in.
fsAnnounce.body2: The integration is in development and coming soon. Meanwhile, GEDCOM import/export and every existing feature remain fully available.

start.title: How do you want to start your tree?
start.intro: Choose how to build your database. You can switch anytime later.
start.fsDesc: Sign in on FamilySearch's own page, then import your tree with everything: photos, notes, sources. Sync features turn on.
start.manualDesc: Start with an empty tree or import a GEDCOM file. FamilySearch features can be enabled later in Settings.
start.confirmIntro: Confirm your choice — it determines how this family tree works.
start.fsP1: Sign in on FamilySearch's own page — TreeMonk never sees your password.
start.fsP2: Your tree imports with EVERYTHING: portraits, photos, notes, sources, occupations, events.
start.fsP3: Per-person refresh from FamilySearch with a preview modal (what will change).
start.fsP4: Background watching: the tree view flags FamilySearch-side changes.
start.fsRestriction: Important: in FamilySearch mode importing a GEDCOM file is NOT possible (export is). The mode can later be changed only with a new family tree or by wiping the database.
start.mP1: Start with an empty tree or import a GEDCOM file.
start.mP2: Everything works locally: trees, investigation board, maps, statistics.
start.mP3: GEDCOM import and export are available anytime.
start.mP4: Your data lives only on your own machine.
start.mRestriction: Important: in Manual/GEDCOM mode the FamilySearch features (sign-in, import, sync) are NOT available. The mode can later be changed only with a new family tree or by wiping the database.
start.fsComingDesc: FamilySearch integration is coming soon. For now, start with Manual / GEDCOM mode.

reimport.title: Important: FamilySearch updates
reimport.warning: If your database was made with an earlier TreeMonk version, FamilySearch features require re-importing your tree (the old import lacks some data). In Manual/GEDCOM mode everything keeps working unchanged.
reimport.fsDesc: Sign in now — then re-import your tree so everything (photos, notes, sources, sync) works.
reimport.manualDesc: Everything stays as it is. FamilySearch features can be enabled later in Settings.

fsScan.title: FamilySearch – scan for changes
fsScan.intro: Read-only: we walk every FamilySearch-linked person and check where data changed, new data appeared, or a person was deleted. Nothing is imported — you choose what to apply.
fsScan.allUpToDate: Everything is up to date — no changes on FamilySearch.

fsImport.hint: The import runs in the background — watch your tree grow meanwhile.

fsExpand.title: Pull in {{name}}'s environment
fsExpand.intro: Deepen the tree around {{name}}: ancestors, descendants, spouses and collateral relatives — to the chosen depth. Runs in the background while the tree grows.
fsExpand.keepsRoot: Your starting person does NOT change — the tree just grows.

preloader.tagline: Your family, from roots to canopy

atlas.noPlaces: No geocoded places for this person.
atlas.emptyHint: Place names need coordinates — run geocoding to pin them.

researchView.title: Research notes
researchView.empty: No research notes yet. Add them on a person’s "Research" tab.

plugins.title: Plugins
plugins.panelUnavailable: The plugin panel is unavailable (the plugin is disabled or missing).
plugins.installed: “{{name}}” installed. Flip the toggle to enable it.
plugins.removeConfirm: Remove the “{{name}}” plugin? Its files are deleted permanently.
plugins.none: No plugins installed. Plugins are sandboxed panels that access your data exclusively through the local API, with their own permissions.
plugins.securityNote: Plugins run sandboxed: they can only connect to the local API with their own scoped token — no external network access, so your data cannot leave this machine. After installing, enable a plugin if you accept the permissions shown below.
plugins.manageDesc: Enable installed plugins, review their permissions and remove them.

wizard.title: Add plugin
wizard.step1Intro: Before you install, here is what to know:
wizard.reqSandbox: Plugins run in a sandboxed panel: they can only reach the local API with their own scoped token — no external network, so your data cannot leave this machine.
wizard.reqLangs: A valid plugin provides its description and menu entries in three languages (Hungarian, English, German) — the installer rejects it otherwise.
wizard.reqTheme: It must follow BOTH light and dark themes — automatic with the official style kit.
wizard.reqZip: A plugin is a single .zip file (with a manifest.json inside). After installing it is listed disabled.
wizard.step2Intro: Pick the plugin's .zip file. The installer validates the manifest — if it is invalid, we show exactly what is wrong.
wizard.errorHint: The message above is for the developer: this is exactly what to fix in manifest.json. Requirements: PLUGINS.md in the TreeMonk repo.
wizard.successBody: Review the requested permissions before enabling. Enabling grants them — you can revoke at any time by disabling the plugin.
wizard.enabled: “{{name}}” enabled — find it in the Plugins menu.

plugins.copiedForLlm: The full plugin specification is on the clipboard — paste it to an AI model.
plugins.copyForLlmDesc: Tip: the “Copy for AI” button puts the ENTIRE technical specification (manifest rules, SDK, full API reference, a working example) on the clipboard as one document — paste it into an AI assistant (e.g. Claude), describe what your plugin should do, and get a ready-made plugin.
